title: Dog Soft Fatty Lump on Ribcage: Lipomas and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Learn about soft fatty lumps (lipomas) on your dog's ribcage, urgency levels, and why documenting the growth with photos is essential for care.
content_html:
TL;DR: A soft, moveable lump on your dog's ribs is likely a benign lipoma, which is usually not an emergency. Monitor for changes in size or texture and consult your vet for a fine-needle aspirate to confirm the diagnosis.

What is a soft fatty lump on my dog's skin?

A soft, moveable, and painless lump under your dog's skin—particularly along the ribcage—is most commonly a lipoma. Lipomas are benign (non-cancerous) tumors composed entirely of fat cells. They are very common in middle-aged and senior dogs. While they are typically harmless, they can occasionally grow large enough to cause discomfort or restrict movement.

Is a soft fatty lump on my dog's ribs an emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, a fatty lump on the ribs is not an emergency.
  • Monitoring: You should monitor the site closely for rapid growth or changes in texture from soft to firm.
  • When to call a vet: Schedule a routine appointment if the skin over the lump becomes red or ulcerated.
  • Diagnosis: A veterinarian will perform a physical exam and a fine-needle aspirate to confirm the diagnosis.

How does taking a photo help my veterinarian triage the lump?

  • Visual Assessment: Photos allow the vet to assess the location and surface appearance of the lump.
  • Tracking Growth: Keeping a photo log over time helps determine the exact rate of growth.
  • Surgical Planning: Growth rate is a critical factor in deciding whether surgical removal is necessary.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Lipomas in dogs typically appear as soft, occasionally pedunculated, discrete nodular masses and are often freely movable; greater than 5% are multiple. These tumors tend to enlarge over time and can be indistinguishable from infiltrative lipomas or liposarcomas based on gross presentation. Lipomas are more common in older, obese female dogs, particularly Doberman Pinschers, Labrador Retrievers, Miniature Schnauzers, and mixed-breed dogs, developing most commonly on the trunk and proximal limbs. A rare variant, diffuse lipomatosis, has been identified in Dachshunds. Excision is generally curative, and dietary restriction before surgery may help define surgical margins.

Chapter: Oncology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 958)

faqs.question: Can a lipoma turn into cancer?
faqs.answer: True lipomas are benign and do not turn into malignant cancer, but they can be difficult to distinguish from more serious growths without a veterinary test called a fine-needle aspirate.

faqs.question: Should I try to squeeze the lump?
faqs.answer: No, you should never squeeze or attempt to 'pop' a lump on your dog. This can cause inflammation, pain, or a secondary infection if the lump is not what it appears to be.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to fatty lumps?
faqs.answer: Yes, while any dog can develop them, breeds like Labrador Retrievers, Weimaraners, and Doberman Pinschers are statistically more likely to develop lipomas as they age.
